label: Brussels — EU Quarter
sub: Home to the European Commission, European Council, and Council of the EU. The Berlaymont building houses Commission headquarters; the Europa building hosts European Council summits.

label: Strasbourg — European Parliament
sub: The official seat of the European Parliament under the EU Treaties, where plenary sessions are held 12 times per year. The city's symbolic position on the Franco-German border reflects its role in European reconciliation.

label: Luxembourg City — ECJ & Court of Auditors
sub: Seat of the European Court of Justice (EU's highest court), the General Court, the Court of Auditors, and the European Investment Bank. Luxembourg was an original ECSC member and key EU institutional hub.

label: Frankfurt — European Central Bank
sub: Headquarters of the European Central Bank (ECB), responsible for the monetary policy of the 20 Eurozone member states. The ECB was established in 1998 and set interest rates for over 340 million citizens.

label: Schengen — Border-Free Zone Birthplace
sub: The small Luxembourg village where the Schengen Agreement was signed on June 14, 1985 aboard a riverboat on the Moselle River. The Schengen Area now comprises 29 European countries with no systematic border controls.

label: Maastricht — Treaty of EU (1992)
sub: The Dutch city where the Treaty on European Union was signed on February 7, 1992, creating the European Union from the European Communities and establishing the path to monetary union and the euro.

label: Rome — Founding Treaties (1957)
sub: The Treaties of Rome establishing the EEC and Euratom were signed at the Palazzo dei Conservatori on the Capitoline Hill on March 25, 1957. Rome periodically hosts key EU anniversary ceremonies.

label: Paris — Treaty of Paris (ECSC, 1951)
sub: The Treaty of Paris establishing the European Coal and Steel Community was signed in Paris on April 18, 1951 — the first act of European supranational integration. The ECSC expired in 2002 after 50 years.

label: Athens — Greece Accession 1981
sub: Greece became the 10th EEC member on January 1, 1981, the first Mediterranean country to join. Its accession was linked to consolidating democracy after the military junta (1967–1974) and was followed by a period of structural fund support.

label: Madrid — Spain Accession 1986
sub: Spain joined the EEC on January 1, 1986 after long negotiations following the death of Francisco Franco in 1975 and democratic transition. Spain became one of the EU's largest recipients of Cohesion Fund support and a major engine of integration.

label: Warsaw — Poland Joins EU (2004)
sub: Poland was the largest of the ten countries in the historic 2004 eastern enlargement. It has since become one of the EU's largest economies and a major recipient of structural funds, though its relations with EU institutions over rule of law have been contentious.

label: Berlin — Wall Falls (1989)
sub: The fall of the Berlin Wall on November 9, 1989 transformed European integration's context, enabling German reunification (October 1990) and opening the prospect of large-scale EU enlargement to the east.

label: London — Brexit (2016–2020)
sub: The UK voted 52% to leave the EU on June 23, 2016 and formally departed on January 31, 2020. The Brexit process was the EU's first treaty withdrawal under Article 50 and prompted major debates about EU reform and future enlargement.

label: Kyiv — Ukraine EU Candidacy (2022)
sub: Ukraine was granted EU candidate status in June 2022 following Russia's full-scale invasion. Accession negotiations were formally opened in June 2024. Ukraine must implement major reforms while simultaneously managing a war on its territory.

label: Belgrade — Western Balkans Candidacy
sub: Serbia has been an EU candidate since 2012 with accession negotiations ongoing. The Western Balkans accession process — covering Serbia, Montenegro, North Macedonia, Albania, Bosnia & Herzegovina, and Kosovo — has stalled repeatedly due to unresolved bilateral disputes and rule of law concerns.

label: Ankara — Turkey Candidacy (Stalled)
sub: Turkey has been an EU candidate since 1999 and began accession negotiations in 2005, but talks have been effectively frozen since 2018 due to rule of law backsliding under President Erdoğan. The EU Parliament has voted to suspend negotiations.

label: Chișinău — Moldova EU Candidacy
sub: Moldova received EU candidate status in June 2022 alongside Ukraine. Accession negotiations were opened in June 2024. Moldova's EU path is proceeding faster than Ukraine's given its smaller size and advanced alignment under President Maia Sandu's reform agenda.

label: Zagreb — Croatia Accession (2013)
sub: Croatia became the EU's 28th member on July 1, 2013, the last enlargement to date. It joined the Schengen Area in January 2023 and adopted the euro in January 2023 — the fastest euro adoption by a new member state.

label: Lisbon — Treaty of Lisbon (2007)
sub: The Treaty of Lisbon was signed in the Jerónimos Monastery on December 13, 2007 and entered into force December 1, 2009 after Ireland's second referendum. It created the permanent European Council President and High Representative roles.

label: Athens — Eurozone Debt Crisis (2010–2018)
sub: Greece received three consecutive bailouts (€110B in 2010, €130B in 2012, €86B in 2015) from the EU/IMF/ECB 'Troika' in exchange for severe austerity measures. The crisis reduced Greek GDP by 26% and sparked a wider Eurozone sovereign debt crisis.

label: Budapest — Rule of Law Crisis
sub: Hungary under PM Viktor Orbán has been at the centre of EU rule of law disputes since 2010. The EU triggered Article 7(1) proceedings against Hungary in 2018 and withheld billions in EU funds under the Conditionality Regulation. Hungary vetoed multiple EU decisions on Ukraine aid and sanctions.

label: Nice — Treaty of Nice (2001)
sub: The Treaty of Nice was signed on February 26, 2001 reforming EU voting weights and institutions ahead of eastern enlargement. Negotiated at a contentious European Council summit, it was initially rejected by Irish voters in 2001 before being approved in a second referendum in 2002.

label: Amsterdam — Treaty of Amsterdam (1997)
sub: The Treaty of Amsterdam was signed on October 2, 1997, incorporating Schengen into EU law, expanding EU powers in justice and home affairs, and strengthening fundamental rights provisions. It entered into force on May 1, 1999.

label: Sofia — Bulgaria Accession (2007)
sub: Bulgaria joined the EU on January 1, 2007 alongside Romania, under a special Cooperation and Verification Mechanism for judicial reform. It joined Schengen fully in January 2025 after completing air/sea access in 2024.

label: Tallinn — EU Digital Innovation Hub
sub: Estonia (joined EU 2004) is Europe's most digitally advanced state, pioneering e-governance and digital identity solutions widely adopted as models for EU digital policy. Tallinn hosted the 2017 EU Presidency Digital Summit.
